summary: O'Neil analyzes the Chicago Police Department Task Force Report, highlighting data that reveals systematic racial bias in policing. The report documents how young black males are stopped at rates nearly equal to their total population, while whites are only stopped when contraband probability is much higher. Despite massive numbers of complaints against officers, 40% go uninvestigated and only 7% are sustained. The post excerpts key findings showing that community distrust of CPD is justified by substantial evidence, the police accountability system is fundamentally broken, and collective bargaining agreements have codified the 'code of silence' into official policy.
key_points: Black and young Chicago residents are disproportionately stopped by police, with young black males stopped at rates nearly matching their total population
Whites are only stopped when the probability of finding contraband is much higher, revealing a clear double standard
The police accountability system fails at every level: 40% of complaints uninvestigated, only 7% sustained, and discipline rarely imposed
Police union contracts actively obstruct accountability by allowing officers to wait 24 hours before statements, confer with others, and amend statements after viewing evidence
The oversight agency IPRA has been run by former law enforcement who allowed findings to be reversed without record
money_quotes: The community's lack of trust in CPD is justified.
The total number of stops for young black males is nearly as large as the population of young black males.
Whites are only stopped when the probability of 'finding contraband' is much higher.
The collective bargaining agreements between the police unions and the City have essentially turned the code of silence into official policy.
Chicago's police accountability system is broken.
Children in some areas of the City are not only being raised in high-crime environments, but they are also being mistreated by those who have sworn to protect and serve them.
In 2014, only 3 out of every 1,000 arrestees had an attorney at any point while in police custody.
key_insight: Data reveals that Chicago's policing crisis isn't just about individual bad actors but about a systematically broken accountability structure where union contracts, oversight failures, and institutional bias work together to shield misconduct.
